pattern: Matching s2 greedily through repeated copies of s1 eventually revisits the same alignment state; detecting that cycle lets you fast-forward through millions of s1 copies arithmetically instead of scanning every character.
hints: Scan s1 once and, for each starting offset into s2, record how the offset advances and how many full s2 copies complete after consuming one whole s1.
After at most a handful of s1 blocks the (offset-into-s2) state must repeat, because there are only len(s2) possible offsets, so the progress per block becomes periodic.
Find the pre-cycle and cycle in terms of (s2-offset after each s1 block), multiply the cycle's s2-count by how many cycles fit in n1, add the remainder, then divide the total s2 matches by n2.

edge_inputs.description: classic case with extra junk characters in s1
s2 character never appears in s1 yields zero
n2 larger than achievable copies forces a smaller answer
s2 longer than s1 needs multiple blocks per match
interleaving so only partial matches accumulate slowly
large repetition count exercises the fast-forward path

stress.description: small random strings with modest repetition counts the brute oracle can simulate
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    alpha = 'abc'
    s1 = ''.join(rng.choice(alpha) for _ in range(rng.randint(1, 8)))
    s2 = ''.join(rng.choice(alpha) for _ in range(rng.randint(1, 4)))
    n1 = rng.randint(1, 60)
    n2 = rng.randint(1, 4)
    return [s1, n1, s2, n2]
stress.note: lengths and counts kept small so char-by-char simulation stays fast

stress.description: two-letter alphabet to force frequent partial matches and cycles
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    alpha = 'ab'
    s1 = ''.join(rng.choice(alpha) for _ in range(rng.randint(2, 10)))
    s2 = ''.join(rng.choice(alpha) for _ in range(rng.randint(1, 3)))
    n1 = rng.randint(1, 80)
    n2 = rng.randint(1, 3)
    return [s1, n1, s2, n2]
stress.note: dense matches stress the cycle detection against brute simulation
